coredata.dc:description: The impact of the carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) wave in agro-application has been growing in recent years. The carbon nanomaterials family includes graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT), carbon nanofiber (CNF), carbon nanohorns (CNH), carbon nanodots (CND), carbon nano-onions (CNO), fullerene, and nano-diamond. The former seven belong to the sp2 carbon and the later belong to sp3 carbon. Based on the selection of the forms of allotropes, morphology, size, and the potential combination to form hybrids; the CNMs have shown different performance in the agro activities. Hence, in this review, all these studies along with our contributions are detailed briefly with the information about material synthesis, characterization, and application efficiency; the application includes the role in pesticide, fertilizer, and preservative sector. The review is finally concluded with the highlights about advantages, gaps/risks identified for the industry application, quality assurance status, and future perspectives.

head.ce:title: Promise of nano-carbon to the next generation sustainable agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The impact of the carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) wave in agro-application has been growing in recent years. The carbon nanomaterials family includes graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT), carbon nanofiber (CNF), carbon nanohorns (CNH), carbon nanodots (CND), carbon nano-onions (CNO), fullerene, and nano-diamond. The former seven belong to the sp2 carbon and the later belong to sp3 carbon. Based on the selection of the forms of allotropes, morphology, size, and the potential combination to form hybrids; the CNMs have shown different performance in the agro activities. Hence, in this review, all these studies along with our contributions are detailed briefly with the information about material synthesis, characterization, and application efficiency; the application includes the role in pesticide, fertilizer, and preservative sector. The review is finally concluded with the highlights about advantages, gaps/risks identified for the industry application, quality assurance status, and future perspectives.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, agro-nanotechnology is gaining significant importance and started to show potential impact in farming activities by improving crop protection and production efficiency [1]. New advances in nanomaterials (NMs) regarding optical, magnetic, and electrical properties are also shifting the regular carrier-based efficiency improvement to the next level of smart agriculture [2,3]. Apart from the production and protection, the scope of the nanotechnological applications in the field of agriculture includes crop improvement technologies (breeding and gene manipulation), preservatives and post-harvest processing, and modified packaging for agricultural commodities [4–8]. The overall goal of using NMs in agriculture is to provide more benign, cost-effective, and healthy food, through sustainable agricultural practices and production [9].

In modern farming, chemicals play a vital role in two important aspects of agriculture viz., crop production and protection in the form of fertilizer and pesticide, respectively. Present-day commercial formulations include emulsion, microemulsion, emulsifiable concentrate, wettable powder, water dispersible granule, suspension concentrate, etc., which are in the range of micron to mm (not less than 2 μm). Currently, the commonly used micronutrients are non-renewable resources, which increases the cost of crop production due to their fast release [10], and a part of nutrients in those non-renewable fertilizers leach into surface water or groundwater. Thus, this phenomenon not only reduces the availability of nutrients to the plants but also cause irreparable damage to the environment like eutrophication [11–14]. In a parallel way, the majority of the applied pesticide loss occurs due to field drift [15], while the rest factors viz., uneven distribution, volatilization, leaching, runoff, and repeated use pose great risks to the human health and environment [16–18]. Thus, more than 90% of the applied active ingredients in the agricultural field go to waste, and agricultural pollution being a nonpoint source of pollution, intensifies the problem than industrial waste [19]. With the advent of nanotechnology, there is a huge hope in the agricultural sector that the size reduction in the above formulations can provide more even agrochemicals distribution and stability against loss through slow, controlled, and anti-drift release formulation. Nanoformulations prove to be promising as they increase the target efficiency by covering more crop surface area [20].

Post-harvest processing is another criterion, which has also become an important element for foods in the recent past. Food products can be spoiled by oxygen, moisture, heat, and light. Therefore, packaging plays an important role to preserve food from chemical, biological, physical, and microbiological spoilage to increase shelf-life; thus, reducing the loss during storage and transportation [21]. Recently, nanocomposites have also become a new trend in the development of food packaging [22]. Biodegradable food packaging promises a more sustainable future in agro-food industries [23].

Many nano-formulations with the metal, metal oxide, metal sulphide, polymer, and carbon-based NMs are developed for the agro-application viz., production, protection, and preservation. In the past 2–3 decades, several allotropes of carbon NMs (CNMs) like CNTs, graphene (oxide form), nano-diamond, nano-onions, fullerene, nano-fiber, mesoporous carbon (MC), and carbon nanodots were employed for agro-applications. At the same time, some of the allotropes are not explored as equally as others [24,25]. CNMs vary from amorphous to crystalline, in crystallinity sp2 and sp3 forms contain different folding and surface functional groups. They are inert compared to the metal composites that often have the phytotoxic effect, and stable compared to polymer nanoparticles (NPs) that often coalesce at the field level as crude practice; which made CNMs suitable for the agro applications [24,25]. But the high surface area and hydrophobic nature of CNMs make strong interaction with leaf and the drug to deliver loss controlled agro formulation [18]. Apart from the direct improvement of efficiency with the controlled delivery, the CNMs are also explored with advanced programmed functions like stimuli-triggered fertilizer. In this queue, its application in the agricultural practice is also studied by us and other counterparts, which has proved to be advantageous. There are few review articles and book chapters has been published related to “carbon nanomaterials in agriculture”, which discussed CNMs in plant gene delivery, genome editing [26] and agro-industrial applications [27–29]. The material has also been explored for the detection of herbicides and pesticides using electrochemical and fluorescence technique [30]. Along with the advantages of CNMs in agriculture, the phytotoxic effects have also been discussed in details by the authors [31]. To our knowledge these literatures are mostly limited to 2017, following which there is many more literature added to this topic which shows the increasing interest in this area (Fig. 1 ). Hence, there is a need to compile these literature published in the past 5 years where some advanced material designs has been developed to demonstrate smart agricultural functions. Further, here we have given special emphasis on the sustainable agriculture in all the sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: History of application of CNMs in different fields
ce:section.ce:para: The development history of CNMs in the field of fertilizers for plant growth and development, pesticides for protection, and food preservation is given in the pictographic form with few representative studies that leads to sustainable agriculture (Fig. 2 ). There are still many more significant studies in this direction, but all the studies cannot bring here. The readers may read the following text for more details.

Therefore, in this article insightful agricultural applications in crop production, protection, and preservation, along with a little bit idea on phytotoxicity effect by different allotropes are discussed. Hence, in this review other NMs for agricultural application will not be discussed, which is beyond the scope, hence the readers may refer to previous reviews in this context [32]. Fig. 3 depicts various applications of carbon nanomaterials in agriculture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different carbon nanomaterials and their applications

ce:section.ce:para: GO is an oxidized form and water-dispersive derivative of graphite, whose synthesis is being pursued with a different oxidation strategy, in which Hummer's method developed in 1958, is a widely accepted route [33].

The unique nature of GO is due to the presence of different oxygen functional groups on the surface and edges of the GO sheets, which makes it hydrophilic and allows for various modifications. The strength of oxidation dictates the number of oxygen groups, and they are distributed as C–O–C, –OH, –CO, and –COO. Uncontrolled chemical reduction of all the functional groups results in partially more hydrophobic material called reduced GO (rGO). Each group has different binding affinity, and the ratio of their distribution can be controlled with the post-synthesis treatment. The efficient binding of molecules, metal ions, and biomacromolecules gives it various functionality and serves as the platform for various applications [34–36]. Many studies have shown that GO sheets are more likely biocompatible with animal and plant cells due to their inert and hydrophilic nature [37,38]. The lignin peroxidise, an enzyme secreted in huge amounts from the white-rot fungi could degrade it efficiently in soil, thus avoiding environmental pollution also [39].

Therefore, many studies have been carried out to explore the use of GO for various bio-applications including drug/gene delivery, biological sensing, and imaging in animal and plant science [40–42]. GO and rGO were also reported to exhibit antibacterial activity [43] and GO being the 2D material has electron transport property too [44].

GO possess ultrahigh strength, high specific area, exceptional mechanical properties, and flexibility [45,46]. The unique properties of graphene-based materials (GBMs) and their capability to improve the mechanical properties of other composite materials with very low dosages inspired many researchers to explore GO-based nanoformulations which are discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GO for fertilizers application and crop production
ce:section.ce:para: To provide crops with sufficient nutrients for maximum productivity, controlled-release fertilizers were developed with the GO. A wide variety of GO formulations were developed, in which some formulation adopts GO coating on the bulk fertilizer, while the others use GO as a nanocarrier for the fertilizer ions/molecules. Finally, in some cases, the GO itself served as the growth physiology stimulant, eventually serving as the fertilizer.

Instead of forming a composite, GO coating was also explored for the controlled release of potassium fertilizer [47]. Here, the potassium nitrate (KNO3) granules were coated with GO and baked at 90 °C, which formed a 20–30 μm coating, thus neglecting the need for conventional polymer coating methods, which need organic solvents and toxic polymerization initiators. The GO coating controlled the potassium release in water and allowed 35% in the initial 7 h, followed by the remaining burst release at 7–8 h. The initial time was spent to establish water channels, which led to the later burst release, thus showing 2-phase controlled-release in reverse to the general initial rapid phase followed by later sustained phase. At the pilot scale with the fluidized bed reactor, KNO3 granules were coated with the latex followed by a layer of GO, and finally with another layer of latex [48]. Here, the hydrophilic nature of the latex stays susceptible to nutrient leaching, hence the partially hydrophobic ∼350 nm GO layer sandwiched between the latex layer causes sustainable release. The increase in the GO thickness was expected to increase the length of the diffusion path, thus the fertilizer is found to release slowly to reduce the loss.

Electronegative potential on the GO, aided it to be suitable for the loading of cationic micro-nutrient and used as a sustainable micronutrient delivery vehicle [10]. The GO was loaded with the micronutrients viz., Zn, and Cu by incubation with the respective sulphate salt solution at pH > 4. The pH is crucial to activate the GO with the negative charge and avoid hydroxide precipitation of Zn and Cu. The loading capacity of micronutrient fertilizers (Zn−GO and Cu−GO) was more than 10% due to the high surface area of GO and high density of oxygen binding sites on the surface and edge of GO. This composite was dried and pelleted, which showed 50% retention after 20 h of soaking. This is highly advantageous and desirable for the crops, where high nutrient loadings are required during initial seedling establishment and at later stages of crop growth, slower and sustained release of micronutrients is needed.

The same research group extended the GO chemistry to form composite granules with monoammonium phosphate (MAP) to overcome abrasion and caking in the conventional formulation [49] (Fig. 4 ), which is a general challenge of the fertilizer industry. Further, in the field application, this problem leads to loss and uneven nutrient distribution. To overcome this, graphene (GN) and GO were tested as the additive in place of the conventional binder at 0.05%–0.5% on MAP fertilizer granule preparation. GO was prepared by improved Hummer's method [50] and GN was prepared by reduction of GO through modified hydrothermal method [51]. Both GO and GN were coagulated with MAP powder at different dosage rates by weight basis. Here, the GN and GO were able to improve the crushing strength of 3 mm granules by 18 times and 16 times, respectively, and abrasion resistance by > 3 times and 5 times respectively, which was attributed to the pore filling ability of the GN, high Young's modulus and intrinsic strength. Fertilizer granules with the GN coating were found to resist 70% humidity without any caking. In the soil diffusion test, the composite with the GN was able to resist diffusion, which will help in the unwarranted loss of fertilizer. In the case of GO additive, an additional oven treatment was found to improve the quality equal to the graphene.

In addition, to serve as the nutrient carrier and slow-release coating for the fertilizer, CNMs were found to aid in the production by serving as a light harvester, redox coupler, micronutrient reservoir, and intra/extra cellular water modulator. In this context, we studied the effect of GO on plant photosynthesis [44] (Fig. 5 ). For this, we prepared 2 types of GO viz., native GO and the other with amine tethered GO. The amine GO (AGO) was prepared by the nucleophilic diethyl amine reaction on electrophilic epoxy groups on the GO surface. Here, we found that the uptake of the AGO was significantly more than the native GO. Further, we hypothesized that in the AGO, since the defects are restored, it may act as an efficient electron shuttled to enhance photosynthesis. But on contrary, we found that the less uptake GO serves as a more efficient photosynthesis modulator, as the aminated GO oxidative toxicity was significant.

Photosynthesis is the fundamental process in plants for production, which is initiated with the electron that can be supplemented with the semiconducting property of GO due to the presence of functional groups on its surface. Further, the redox protein shuttles the synthesized electron to the surface of the thylakoid for the glucose synthesis, which can be partially supported by the redox property of the GO. To check this hypothesis i.e., to verify if the GO, can participate in the photosynthesis, we isolated the chloroplast and incubated it with the GO and aminated GO, which showed that the GO can significantly improve the photosynthesis rate [44]. Whereas, here the aminated GO surprisingly inspite of the high uptake into the chloroplast, confirmed with the confocal and biolayer interferometry, we observed poor photosynthesis, which has been apparently confirmed due to the excess toxicity.

In a similar manner with fertilizers, there is also a need for the controlled release of pesticides to avoid pesticide washing by rain, which causes loss and non-target accumulation. Non-target accumulation leads to the development of insecticide resistance and residual effect on non-target organisms. Hence, intensive and repeated use of insecticides is becoming increasingly restricted, which eventually leads to invalid control of the target insects [52]. To solve these problems, one of the facile approaches is the application of the insecticides with other materials such as GO to formulate new GO–insecticides formulation, having different modes of action against pests [18].

In this context, recently we developed copper selenide (Cu2-xSe) NPs decoration on reduced GO by arrested precipitation (rGO-Cu2-xSe), for the targeted chlorpyrifos (CPF) application [20] (Fig. 6 ). Here, chlorpyrifos was allowed to load on rGO-Cu2-xSe through hydrophobic interaction and transferred to aqueous phase through evaporation assisted wrapping with amphiphilic poly (styrene-maleic acid) polymer. Owing to the hydrophobicity, rGO-Cu2-xSe showed enhanced 3.2 times CPF loading and 2-times antidrift adhesion. The enhanced adhesion furnished 2-times more Pieris rapae pest control efficiency. Further, the release of the pesticide was found to be in a targeted fashion with the alkaline gut pH of larva. Here, the Cu2-xSe complements rGO in the photothermal property to show photo stimuli-based release, whose application is anticipated for diurnal pest control. Following this, we extended the same material rGO-Cu2-xSe as a carrier for fungicide viz., captan application, as copper is known to have a strong fungicidal effect, we envisaged that it may give a complementary effect with the captan [53]. However, experimentally we could only document additive effect, but on the other hand, we observed triple smart behaviour viz., 1. Initial binding to the leaf against drift along with tethering of the fungicide, 2. targeted release of the fungicide with the reduced pH stimuli that simulate the hyphal cytoplasm and 3. Holding the fungicide from leaching into the soil and groundwater pollution by the soil phosphate.

A series of the GO-ZnAl-layered double hydroxide (LDH) composites (CPF/GO-LDH) by an exfoliation-restacking method was prepared to load CPF and used as slow-release pesticides carrier [54]. The composite CPF/GO-LDH displayed the slow-release property of pesticide by surface diffusion (which extended from 24 h to 48 h depending upon the mass ratio of GO/LDH, pH of buffer solution, and the release media). Whereas, the control CPF/GO showed release equilibrium of pesticide within 50 min and the accumulated release amounts were about 50%. Similarly, Hy-GO@PDA (hymexazol loaded in polydopamine coated GO) nanocomposite showed control release of pesticide and prevent pesticide loss by rainwater [55]. The release of Hymexazol from nanocomposite was NIR-laser-dependent and pH-dependent. The inhibition activity of the composite against Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. Cucumebrium was also similar to that of the hymexazol solution. Dynamic contact angle measurement studies showed an increase in contact angle by 11° after 10 min of observation on cucumber leaves in stimulated rain-wash suggesting an increase in hymexazol's persistence when it was loaded in GO@PDA. This composite proved to be a boon, especially for water-soluble pesticides due to the removal of wash-off problems, low-utilization, improved adhesion performance, and controlled release.

The synergistic activity of GO with the insecticides, namely, β-cyfluthrin (Cyf), Monosultap (Mon), and Imidacloprid (Imi) against a lepidoptera insect Asian corn borer (ACB) has been reported [18]. GO was synthesized by Hummer's method [33], and combined with the insecticide at the ratio of 1:2 to form a GO–insecticide formulation. These GO-insecticides (Cyf, Mon, and Imi) formulations exhibited 2.1, 1.51, and 1.83 fold higher contact toxicities, respectively than the insecticides alone. This higher toxicity in addition to the insecticide activity is facilitated due to physical damage of insect's cement layer caused by GO. This physical disruption results in rapid water loss from the insects; hence insecticides can penetrate through this disrupted cement layer and ultimately the GO delivers the insecticide more efficiently. So, GO has been speculated as a new type of ‘green’ synergist for pest management, which overcomes insect resistance due to its multifunctional synergistic mechanism. Taking a lead in this study, very recently, Zhou et al., 2021 showed GO as a carrier for acaricides (Avermectin, Bifenazate, Etoxazole, and Spirodiclofen) [56]. Here, very interestingly, they have confirmed the biocide effect to be due to the mechanical damage to the cuticle of Tetranychus cinnabarinus.

Besides pesticide delivery, GO based composites are also explored as a platform for the removal of pesticides. A nanohybrid comprising of zirconium-based metal organic framework (MOF) and GO were explored for selective organophosphorus pesticide (OPP) removal to reduce the environmental risk, recently [57]. The MOF constructed over GO here belongs to UiO-67 family having Zr as the metal node and N, N′-dimethylformamide as the organic linker. This nanohybrid was synthesized using the hydrothermal method, and the MOF was found to show OPP specificity. This is due to the MOF's remarkable properties like high specific surface area (UiO-67 have 3000 m2/g, which is higher than most reported MOFs) [58], tuneable pore size, and exposed active sites. The UiO-67 is stable and has Zr–OH groups required for the effective interaction or binding of OPP pesticides (glyphosate). GO/UiO67 nanocomposite is found to have more adsorption capacity as compared to GO or any other reported adsorbents. The adhesion performance of glyphosate, a well-known herbicide by the nanohybrid was maximum at pH 4 due to the higher affinity of the Zr–OH towards phosphorus than for H+ ions. Langmuir and Freudlich model verified the specificity of glyphosate to the nanohybrid has been due to the surface/inner complex formation with functional groups of UiO-67/GO. Hence, this nanohybrid system can be used as an excellent adsorbent for the purification of water contaminated with any herbicide or pesticide.

Similarly, a GO-magnetide β-cyclodextrin-MOF (M-MOF) nanocomposite was developed as a potential adsorbent for adsorption and removal of nicotinoid insecticides (thiamethoxam, imidacloprid, acetamiprid, nitenpyram, dinotefuran, clothiandin, and thiacloprid) [59]. In the first step, iron oxide nanoparticles decorated GO was synthesized and tethered with β-cyclodextrin. Following this, the composite was coated with the MOF using copper as the metal node and 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid as the organic linker. The BET surface area and the pore volume of M-MOF were 250.33m2g−1and 0.826 mLg−1 respectively, which vary the adsorption capacity of these insecticides from 1.77 to 3.11 mgg−1. The N-containing groups, hydrophobic groups, and localized large π bond from the benzene ring of these insecticides facilitate the adsorption process by hydrophobic interaction, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interaction with M-MOF. Adsorption binding rate was high in the initial 30 min and equilibrium began to plateau when the total time exceeds 60 min. For real-time study, tap water samples were spiked with different concentrations of nicotinoid insecticides and treated with 20 mg M-MOF. Almost all of the nicotinoid insecticide (0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mg/L) spiked samples were removed by M-MOF suggesting their role as an effective simple adsorbent for water purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GO for food processing and packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Biopolymers are an effective alternative for the conventional petroleum-derived polymers to reduce the environmental threat, as they are biodegradable and are from renewable resources [8].

Keeping this in view, a composite film of impermeable rGO sandwiched between protective poly (lactic acid) (dPLA) was synthesized [23] (Fig. 7 ). Usually, in food packaging, the PLA possesses poor water vapour and oxygen barrier properties compared to other petroleum-derived ones. But the synergy between the core barrier of GO films obtained via pressure-assisted filtration [60] and the protective encapsulation of the PLA to form PLA−graphene composite film with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) binder [61] caused 87.6% reduction in the water vapour permeability. Further, it shows two order reduction of oxygen permeability in both dry and humid conditions. These barrier properties can be due to the compact lamellar microstructure and hydrophobicity of the rGO core barrier. This PLA−GN composite film also exhibited at least eight-fold extension in the shelf-life of edible oil and potato chips, which are oxygen and moisture-sensitive food products.

In the conventional fruit preservation practice, the preservatives were directly applied on the fruit, which is hard to separate during consumption, hence lead to health effects. So, to avoid the contact of preservatives on the fruits, but to make the preservative available on-demand, we developed a GO-based smart wrapper [62] (Fig. 8 ). The GO synthesized by improved Hummer's method [50] was tethered with salicylaldehyde (SA) through hydrazone linkage (SAhyd-GOA). A stable membrane of this composite was prepared through vacuum-filtration with the help of porous filter paper support. The acid-labile hydrazone has been envisaged to cleave by the acid synthesized during the overripening of the fruits. The composite showed − ̴ 50% of SA release in acidic pH; while in neutral pH the composite showed only 15% release from SAhyd-GOA composite after incubation for 50 h. The fermented banana juice collected from post-climacteric fruit exudation having pH 3.2 also showed 11.5% SA release after 5 h incubation. The SA is a natural phenol and a plant immune modulator that controls ripening and has antimicrobial activity [63]. The increase in the acidic content of fruits during the over-ripening causes release of SA from the composite, which acts as a preservative and increases fruit shelf-life. Moreover, malondialdehyde (MDA) content, which is increased in case of ripening and senescence due to the oxidative damage in the cell membrane by lipid peroxidation was found to be decreased by 50% in bananas having SAhyd-GOA composite treatment as compared to untreated banana. This may occur due to the phenolic preservatives ability of SA to control oxidative stress. The antibacterial activity of the composite tested against common food contaminant Escherichia coli and the versatile bacterial plant pathogen species Pseudomonas syringae showed more control than that of the free SA. This is due to the complementary action of the SA with the antibacterial mechanism of GO.

The composite also reduced the cytotoxicity by ∼70%, as compared to free SA when tested against fibroblast cell line (NIH3T3). This is due to the unavailability of the SA to reach the cells freely from the composite. So overall, the final composite showed many advantages like less toxicity, reusable, efficient preservative release with the stimuli of the overripe fruit juice, and improves fruit shelf life.

Another group also checked the antibacterial and preservative properties of a different composite [64], who synthesized a self-assembled film of GO and chitosan (CS) biopolymers with titanium dioxide (TiO2) NPs embedded in its surface (GO@CS@TiO2 nanocomposite) (Fig. 9 ). GO was synthesized by modified Hummer's method from flake graphite and decorated with TiO2 using titanium isopropoxide as the precursor. The treatment with 40 μg/mL nanocomposite for 12 h was sufficient to inhibit Bacillus subtilis survival and reproduction. Whereas to inhibit 95% of mycelial growth in Aspergillus niger, 200 μg/mL nanocomposite was required indicating that the antibacterial effect of GO@CS@TiO2 nanocomposites was greater than the antifungal effect. Particularly, 1:20:4 ratio of GO, CS, and TiO2 NPs in the nanocomposites exhibited high antibacterial activity. Efficient cell damage was confirmed through conductivity, Coomassie brilliant blue G-250 experiments, and fluorescent assays. The sharp edges of GO sheets caused a release of proteins and cell contents to show antibacterial properties. Also, the composite stayed safe without showing cytotoxicity against mammalian somatic cells and plant cells. The HEK 2931T cells incubated with 10 and 80 μg/mL concentration of the composite for 24 h remained with >90% and >80% viability, respectively. Parallelly, the growth of nanocomposite treated plants was found higher than the untreated plants. Thus, the synthesized nanocomposite disrupted microbial film formation selectively by avoiding significant damage to animal and plant cells, which makes it biocompatible.

In the fruit preservation experiment, no composite coated fruits and vegetables showed less than 5% weight loss in seven days but the weight loss of the untreated group was very high. The polyphenol oxidase (PPO) activity (responsible for fruit browning) was lowered and superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity (responsible for inhibition of lipid peroxidation) was higher in nanocomposite treated groups. This suggests that the nanocomposite has the potential to be used as food preservatives. Thus, the proposed nanocomposite has a potential to be used as a fruit wrapper for a longer shelf-life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of GO in sustainable agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed, GO act as an inert platform for sustainable development in agriculture field by avoiding leaching, wash-off problems of fertilizers, pesticides, herbicides, micronutrients. Hence indicated their sustained, controlled and targeted release. All these applications of GO leads to decrease in soil as well as water pollution [65] and also reduces burden on microbial abundance, thus contributing in sustainable agriculture.

Further recently in the search for sustainable agriculture, we have prepared nanocarbon-matrix for pheromone application and pest control as an eco-friendly alternative of use of pesticides [66]. (Fig. 10 ). The burst release nature of semi-chemical volatiles reduces its field efficacy to short span. Keeping this in view, a mutil-layer GO synthesized by improved Hummer's method [50], and amine conjugated GO by the nucleophilic ethyl diamine (EDA) attack on the electrophilic epoxide ring of GO, was used to construct an edifice for the slow release. The tomato pin worm (Tuta absoluta) pheromone on carbon based materials having high saturation capacity adsorbs volatiles and cause slow release of pheromones without altering the composition.

The field efficacy of the nano-matrix with pheromones showed 1015 and 781 pests trapped in 1 mg loaded GO and AGO respectively which was greater in number as compared to commercially used septa containing 3 mg of pheromone leading to sustainable agriculture practice.

CNTs were discovered by Sumio Iijima in 1991 [67]. CNTs are an allotrope of carbon, considered as a transition between fullerene and GN sheets. These are the rolled GN sheets joined by a covalent bond (sp2) in a hexagonal pattern to form a semiconducting hollow cylindrical piece. They can be arranged as single-hollow and multi-hollow concentric cylinder/s, are categorized as single-wall nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multiwalled nanotubes (MWCNTs), respectively [68]. On comparing, MWCNTs are much cheaper to produce as compared to SWCNT.

The unique properties of CNTs are seen to arise a range of applications in the field of nanobiotechnology by using it as a nanocomposite [69]. The major share of CNTs interest in different purposes like plant germination, growth, and gene expression studies comes from their inimitable mechanical, electrical, thermal, and chemical properties [3]. A number of researchers have reported the dramatic effect of MWCNTs on plant growth and development not only by enhancing the water uptake ability but also have a positive effect on gene expression [70–73]. Similarly, a boosted germination rate and growth were reported in wheat and alfalfa [74].

The excellent mechanical properties [75], high tensile strength, and elastic modulus [76] make it to use as a filler material for polymer nanocomposites i.e., polymer's reinforcing agent [77]. The CNTs polymer composites have an extensive application in the field of electrochemistry based sensor, because of its ability to promote charge transfer [78–80].

Altering the functional groups on the surface of CNTs facilitates an improved performance in specific reactions [81]. Wang et al., 2014 reported that MWCNT with –COOH surface groups, CC, and carbonyl groups exhibit high binding efficiency. This modification turned the MWCNTs to hydrophilic, which made it easier to disperse into dimethylformamide (DMF) to prepare a uniform electrode film for estrogen measurement and food packing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNTs for plant growth and development
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs are known to have its role as a growth regulator, which is discussed in subsequent text.

MWCNTs were synthesized using graphite as the substrate and Fe:Co:CaCO3 (3.35:1.65:95) as a catalyst using chemical vapour deposition (CVD) technique at a temperature of 720 °C in the presence of nitrogen gas [82] (Fig. 11 ). Following this, the effect of CNTs on the germination of monocot and dicot was tested at 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, and 200 μg/mL concentration in the MS medium (Murashige and Skoog). CNTs led to an accelerated germination process in two monocots (corn and barley) and one dicot (soybean) that caused 100% germination in 6 days incubation, whereas the control plants reached only 63% germination. Apparently, this improvement in the germination was found due to the increased expression of aquaporin channels (LeAqp2) in the treated samples confirmed with RT-PCR test, which is documented in tomato seedlings before as well [71]. To further explore the application of the CNTs on the seed treatment, the CNTs were sprayed on seed surface at three varied concentrations i.e. 25 μg/mL (light spray), 50 μg/mL (medium spray), and 100 μg/mL (heavy spray). In these concentrations, the maximum germination was observed in the case of 100 μg/mL. The control seedlings showed no physical signs of germination on a post-treatment day in comparison to MWCNTs supplemented media. The presence of MWCNTs inside these seeds were confirmed using Raman spectroscopy (785 nm laser for the detection of specific G and 2D band to evaluate crystallinity of these nanostructures in the range of 1600 cm−1 to 2600 cm−1, respectively) as well as with TEM.

MWCNTs composite with zinc oxide NPs (ZnO/MWCNT) was developed to check the effect on the onion seed germination and growth [83]. MWCNTs served as a dual-purpose vehicle that can keep the ZnO stable and participate in cell penetration. The dual vehicle also found to be smart by releasing the ZnO in a sustainable fashion. The synergistic role of Zn and MWCNTs to enhance the growth of onion seed under arid conditions was found due to the adequate supply of Zn and enhanced water uptake. The adequate supply of the Zn was confirmed while following the negative control, where ZnO/MWCNTs were treated under succinic acid incubation. The succinic acid formed a tetrahedral complex with Zn and turned the composite hydrophilic, which limited the penetration and availability of the Zn and reduce germination and growth. The ZnO/MWCNTs improved the germination percentage by 1.5 folds within three days at 15 μg/mL of ZnO/MWCNTs treatment under limited water availability. This treatment also caused the maximum number of cells in telophase stage that confirmed increased cell division rate.

In another study, the effect of CNT and activated carbon at 50 μg/mL carbon particles concentration in the MS plant culture medium was tested on tomato plants [73]. CNTs showed better biomass production, while the activated carbon doesn't show any difference from the control. Further analysis of the CNTs treated tomato plant with the photothermal and photoacoustic signal confirmed the particles distribution in the root and leaf. Microarray technique showed that CNTs regulated a set of genes involved in stress responses, cellular processes, transport, catabolic, metabolic, and biosynthetic processes. Further qPCR analysis of the selected stress (heat shock and TDR3) and physiological (water channel protein) gene revealed that it has got upregulated. Following the above observation, CNTs were confirmed to tune the soil microbial population with increasing bacteroidetes and reducing proteobacteria. Here, the reproduction growth viz., the flowering and fruit yield in tomato was found to double compared to the activated carbon and control.

However, some researchers have reported deleterious effects of MWCNTs on plants especially on the nitrogen assimilation activity of leguminous plants [84]. Wang et al., in 2017 demonstrated a negative effect of MWCNTs on nitrogen fixation of soybean and its nodulation process. Unexpectedly, a stronger inhibitory effect was found at lower concentrations than in high concentration, as it is relatively more dispersed and bio-accessible in comparison to high concentration treatment. Similarly, another study by the same group showed that MWCNTs (average outer diameter of 18.8 ± 4.1 nm and a length of 10–30 μm) exhibit a contrary result on soybean nitrogen fixation [85] but had a positive effect on soil nitrogen assimilation, therefore, overall plant nitrogen stoichiometry was not compromised. Here, the enhanced nitrogen assimilation counterpoises the reduced nitrogen fixation by the MWCNTs. This leads to an increase in the demand of nitrogen fertilizers in order to maintain crop production, hence disturbing the soil-plant health.

In the GO section on plant production, we discussed about our work on the documentation of the effect of GO on photosynthesis, which is a motivation from the Giraldo et al., 2014 work on the effect of photosynthesis with the addition of CNT [86]. Here, they have meticulously observed that CNT has the ability to improve photosynthesis by 3 times.

As a nutrient and nutrient carrier, the CNTs were found to play an important role in the above section, similarly, the role of CNTs as the pesticide carrier and crop protection is narrated below. In this context, the uptake of four organochlorine insecticides viz., chlordane (cis-chlordane [CS], transchlordane [TC] and trans-nonachlor [TN]) and p,p’-DDE (all at 100 ng/L), by lettuce (Lactuca sativa Linn.) with the CNTs as the carrier has been studied [87]. For this experiment, unfunctionalized and amino-functionalized CNTs have been used. Functionalized MWCNTs possessed a length of 10–13 μm and diameter of less than 8 nm, whereas the amino-functionalized CNTs had a length of 3–30 μm and diameter of 13–18 nm. Lettuce crop was selected for this study due to its advantages like higher germination rate, short vegetative cycle, sensitivity to contaminants, and low genetic variability. Lettuce seeds sown in the CNTs mixed vermiculite medium showed even growth, following this the pesticide was added along with the irrigation. In the case of non-functionalized CNTs, the uptake of pesticide residue decreased by 85–90% in the case of CS, TC, TN, and DDE. But there has been an increase in the pesticide uptake in the treatment with NH2 functionalized CNTs by approximately 3.4–4 times than the non-functionalized MWCNTs. Further, the study was extended to check the second factor viz., humic acid (HA). HA in the presence of non-functionalized CNTs showed no significant change in the pesticide accumulation in roots; whereas in the presence of HA, the amino functionalized MWCNTs significantly increased the pesticide accumulation in roots. This may possibly due to strong electrostatic interaction between HA group and NH2 functionalized CNTs, thus HA could mask and enhance the navigation of the CNTs into the root; similarly improved HA composites were already reported in for the phthalate transport in the plants [88]. The enumeration of the chlorophyll content also supported the fact that the MWCNTs interaction was not toxic. The uptake of pesticides by the plants is all dependent on the type of crop, the NMs used, their concentration, which could have economic implications for growers adding hydrophobic agrichemicals for pest control [89].

MWCNTs on the tobacco (Nicotiana benthamiana) plant were found to suppress the infection caused by Turnip mosaic virus (TuMV) [90]. In this study, the performance of MWCNT was compared with iron oxide, titania, and fullerene at 50 and 200 mg/L concentrations. The control and the treated plants were infected with green fluorescence protein-tagged TuMV and monitored by fluorescence imaging. Here, the performance of the material at higher concentrations was poor compared to the low concentration, which is due to their aggregation. To understand the mechanism, RNA and protein levels were quantified, which showed that the later translational stage got affected. The control in the virus correlated with the hormone especially with the stress hormone abscisic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNTs for food processing and packaging
ce:section.ce:para: An antimicrobial film composed of CNTs was developed and its application for food preservation was tested [21]. Low-density polyethylene film comprising of xylene and CNTs were fabricated with different concentrations. The mixer was thoroughly homogenized and ultra-sonicated followed by overnight drying and vacuum-assisted evaporation. The ability of the film to preserve Mazafati dates was tested, which are prone to microbial contamination due to its high moisture and sugar content. No significant physiological difference could be observed on mazafati dates incubated with CNTs composed films even after 60 days. While the control dates without any packing got spoiled and the degree of spoilage increased on a logarithmic scale due to the accumulation of bacteria and fungus. Nanotube concentration based mortality due to the perforation of the E. coli was observed [91]. Thereby, increasing the shelf-life and preserving the integrity and constituents of food. In an another study, PLA/CNTs/CS composite fibers containing different CS contents was fabricated through electrospinning and the ability of these fibers on strawberry preservation was examined [92]. The results showed that 7 wt % CS content had better solubility, swelling ratio, mechanical and antimicrobial properties against gram positive and gram negative bacteria. The addition of small amount of CNTs in the composite increased the biocompatability and mechanical performance of the fiber, which reduced the problem of brittleness in PLA/CS composite [93]. Strawberry preservation experiment for 8 days showed less weight loss, titratable acidity, total soluble solids, ascorbic acid content and more firmness, in wrapped strawberries as compared to control group.

The field of CNTs has not been explored as often as GO, especially in the case of fertilizers, pesticides, food processing, and packaging. More research needs to be done on CNTs to open a new paradigm in the agriculture industry. Few of the researches carried out in the recent past in this field were discussed above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of CNT in sustainable agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: CNTs have been proven to be an attractive option in sustainable agriculture, owing to its benefit in gene regulation, cell penetration, fertilizers/pesticides delivery system and slow release profile. All these benefits makes it capable of reducing the environmental pollution and making ecological balance leading to sustainable agriculture [94].
Other CNMs used in agricultural practices include MC, CNOs, CNDs, CNHs, CNFs, and biochar, etc. which directly or indirectly help in plant growth.

For instance, MC NPs, having a pore size between 2 nm to 50 nm can be synthesized by various means including some synthetic methods of hard and soft templates. The preparation of MC with well-defined pore structures and narrow pore-size distributions [95] enhanced properties like high specific surface area, electronic and ionic conductivity, diffusion, and excellent accessibility to the active site. These properties make them a unique choice to be used in divergent fields like gas storage, biomolecule detection, carbon capture, photocatalysis, vapour sensing, drug delivery, and electrochemical applications [96].

MC platform with the polyethylenimine (PEI) has been shown to demonstrate stimuli controlled release system (CRS) of fertilizer selenite, which is crucial in the selenium deficient soil in China and Europe [97] (Fig. 12 ). Here hollow MC NPs (HMCNPs) was synthesized by HCl washing of the composite arrived after hydrothermal treatment of polyethylene glycol with ferrocene. Following this, the porous tunnels and the outer surface were covered with the polyethyleneimine, which facilitated to hold selenite ions by ionic binding. Since the ionic strength is pH dependent, the maximum loading of ∼40% (w/w) was achieved at pH 3. The composite was able to show stimuli-controlled release based on the availability of carbonate/phosphate and hydroxyl ions in the medium by the ion exchange and deprotonation of the amine in PEI, respectively. In the plant experiment, the phosphate stimulation was able to increase the growth, chlorophyll, and vegetable selenium content.

CNOs are the zero-dimensional CNMs, with multi-layered fullerenes arranged concentrically (C60 or C80 fullerenes at the core), -a structure like an onion. The diameter lies in the range of 1.4 and 50 nm and has an interlayer distance of 3.4 Å, which largely depends on the method of preparation. The low cytotoxicity, hydrophobic nature, and its ability to facilitate facile non-covalent functionalization created an interest, in the field of nanomedicine [98]. This allotrope of carbon has been shown to work as the growth promoter in gram plant [99] (Fig. 10). The germinated gram seedlings were incubated in the CNOs at 10–30 μg/mL and observed to show improved growth, hence the test was forwarded to the soil treatment. Interestingly, in the lateral section of the plant, they were able to observe water soluble CNOs embedded in the xylem vessel. Here, the porosity and the rich functional groups on CNOs acted as the reservoir for the sustainable micronutrient supply in the xylem that suffice their role as growth promoter.

CNDs are the new class of zero-dimensional CNMs with a size below 10 nm. The strong fluorescent property is the main reason behind its wide attention in the field of nanotechnology. CNDs with good chemical stability, photo bleaching resistance, ease of surface functionalization, good water solubility, and feasibility to scale up for large-scale production, stimulate its potential applications in nanomedicine, solar cells, chemical sensors electrocatalysis, and light-emitting diode (LED) [100,101].

The effects of CNDs on the plant was first tested through a mung bean germination test [102]. The CNDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal process of the particles obtained from the electrolysis of the graphite. CNDs at the concentration of 0.02 mg/mL was found to enhance the root and shoot growth compared to the control. The reason for this was correlated with the enhancement in plant water level, root vigour, photosystem 1 activity, rubisco activity, and eventually carbohydrate production.

When the studies on the impact of the CNMs to plants were limited to different allotropes, a complete radial insight was given with the introduction of the chiral dots into mung bean by the same group [103]. The chiral carbon dots were synthesized by the pyrolysis in teflon sealed autoclave, using d-cysteine or l-cysteine; whereas the control was synthesized with dl-cysteine. The mung beanseeds were treated with thus synthesized CNDs at 0–1000 μg/mL concentrations. The D-carbon dots were found to be more effective in promoting root and shoot length at 100 μg/mL, beyond which it starts to retard the growth. At the maximum concentration viz., 1000 μg/mL, the growth was retarded below the control; whereas the concentration between 100 - 1000 μg/mL caused the lesser growth than 100 μg/mL but more than control. The increase in the activity of rubisco enzyme was found to be the reason for the promotion observed with D-carbon dots, which was absent in L-carbon dots and achiral-carbon dots.

The CNHs are the most advanced CNMs having porous, cage like structures and a diameter in the range of 2–5 nm with a length of 40–50 nm. The CNHs prove a significant advantage over CNTs, as the metal catalyst is not involved in the synthesis process [104], further purification steps to remove metal like Ni, Fe, or Co could be skipped/ignored. Also, the CNHs have a high aspect ratio as compared to CNTs allowing more space for the movement of macromolecules inside these structures. The pores can be activated using various treatments like oxidation and CO2 thermal treatments leading to a rapid increase in the surface area thereby leading to varied applications like energy storage, drug delivery, and super capacitors [71]. Another major advantage of using CNHs is the ability to tune the pore size according to the synthesis conditions [105].

The effects of CNHs on the seed germination were studied with the material synthesized by heating carbon sources at 1150 °C using Nd:YAG laser light under inert conditions and at atmospheric pressure [104] (Fig. 13 ). CNHs exposure led to improvement in the seed germination of six different species like barley, corn, soybean, rice, switchgrass, tomato seeds, and also accelerates the growth of tobacco callus culture. These CNHs were also non-toxic even at the concentration of 100 μg/mL. The exposure of callus to 500 μg/mL of MWCNTs lead to 64% increase in growth. However, the SWCNHs were found to stimulate the same effect and boost tobacco callus culture by 78% at five times reduced concentration of MWCNTs (i.e.100 μg/mL) due to the fact that it contains less metal impurities. The uptake of CNHs was observed in the TEM micrograph. The uptake was quantified using the microwave induced heating method as both the CNHs and CNTs to absorb microwave radiation power (30 W) in a short duration of exposure [106].

The influence of SWCNHs of 2–5 nm in diameter and 10–20 nm in length on Arabidopsis wild type Col-0 was also studied [107]. The optimal concentration to boost the plant primary growth and development was found to be 0.1 mg/L as it upregulated the expression of stem cell-related genes i.e. WOX1,WOX5, and cell cycle related gene (CYCLIN -U1-1) which was confirmed by an increase in primary root growth as well as lateral root growth caused due to increase in the length of meristematic zone because of enhanced cell division and proliferation. The genes leading to auxin biosynthesis i.e. YUC3 and YUC5 were found to be upregulated in the roots, which also reverts back to normal after 3–4 days. However, the higher concentrations of the MWCNTs i.e. 50 mg/L and 100 mg/L was found to inhibit the growth and overall development of the plant. Also, the CNHs were proven to upregulate a variety of gene, which are involved in carbon metabolism, terpenoid synthesis, and also levels of several secondary metabolites i.e. nicotinamides, flavones, hypoxanthine, and adenine.

CNFs are the non-continuous filaments with GN layers arranged as stacked cones (linear sp2) exhibit high specific area, flexibility, porosity, and super strength, which qualifies its use in electrode materials for energy storage devices and bone tissue scaffolds. CNFs of 50–200 nm can be obtained by catalytic chemical vapour deposition and electrospinning method [108].

In the aim to nourish the plant with the micronutrient there were attempts to use metal NPs, which often aggregate or show accumulation and toxicity. In this context, CNFs with copper oxide (CuO) NPs (CNF–CuO) were synthesized and applied as copper micronutrient feeding fertilizer composite in Cicer arietinum [109]. CNFs-CuO was prepared by the CVD method using acetylene as the carbon source with the activated carbon (AC) and CuO as the platform. Following this, the resultant was milled and centrifuged to separate 230 nm CNFs-CuO from the activated carbon. CNFs-CuO at the rate of 500 μg/mL showed 98% germination compared to 60% achieved in the control. Apart from the germination enhancement, the plants treated with CNFs-CuO showed well-developed cellular compartment, and good copper translocation efficiency from the root to shoot. The chlorophyll and protein content were improved in CNFs-CuO treatment followed by CNFs, and ACF composites.

To our knowledge, hardly any literature of the nanodiamond (ND) (the sp3 form of carbon) for the agricultural application is reported, except for the following couple of studies. Uptake study that shows that the ND coated with fulvic acid faired well in the accumulation by wheat seedlings than the ND coated with humic acid [110]. In another study, to cope up with the water shortage in the agricultural field, a novel detonation nanodiamond@sodium alginate hydrogel bead (DND@SA) was synthesized using the ion gelation technique. This can be used as a sunlight-controlled release-targeted system for irrigation purpose, however, this study was not tested with the plant [111].

Biochar has attracted considerable attention as a soil amendment in agriculture. Biochar is a promising renewable resource for the management of soil's fertility, due to its environmental and economic benefits [112]. Various mechanisms of biochar like a release of nutrients in the soil, inside plant and improvement of soil microbiological and physiological properties, make it appropriate for various agricultural applications. Several studies found that the addition of biochar with soils could increase porosity, decrease bulk density, improve soil structure, and enhance water retention and aggregation [113]. These qualities of biochar attracted many researchers to explore it further and hence it was prepared by the burning/pyrolysis of the biomass and removing the unburnt plant material by sieving by a group of scientists [114]. The collected carbon particles were ground and dispersed into the water to separate the NPs. In this step, the bulky and the particles with silica and other metal settled down immediately and allowed the pure carbon NPs to float, which was decanted for further use. As part of it was acid-treated to establish hydrophilic carboxyl groups. In the incubation of the wheat plant with the biochar, the biochar with acid treatment performed well with the optimum concentration to be 50 mg/L. To unveil the reason behind the ability of the biochar to stimulate growth, the raw biochar, and acid-treated biochar were incubated with ammonium nitrate and found that they can release the cations and anions sustainably.

Non-activated and concentrated HCl activated biochars were studied for the sorption and desorption of nitrates. Further also studied for the effect of solution pH and presence of highly negative charged ions on sorption [115]. They found that increase in pH and the presence of anions lead to a decrease in the sorption capacity of activated biochar as compared to non-activated. Further, biochar was reported to increase the water-holding capacity of the soil, which in turn lead to yield improvement [116]. Studies reported that the addition of biochar to soil could increase available water capacity from 0.12 to 0.13 m3m−3 [117], and by over 22% [118]. Several studies reported that biochar application could increase the cation exchange property and pH value of soil. Laird et al., 2010 found that biochar application increased the soil pH value by 1 unit [119]. A similar study reported that biochar application leads to an increase in the soil pH from 3.33 to 3.63 [120]. Other reports found that the treatment of soil with biochar significantly enhanced the cation exchange capacity from 4 to 30% [119], and from 7.41 to 10.8 μmolkg−1 in weathered soil [121]. Further, biochar treatment was reported to enhance total nitrogen content from 0.24 to 0.25%, phosphorus content from 15.7 to 15.8 mgkg−1, total carbon content 2.2–2.7% [122] and decrease the rate of soil erosion from 1458 to 532 gm−2h−1 [121]. These improvements in soil chemical properties could increase soil fertility by increasing the nutrient contents and availability. Several studies also reported the influence of biochar on microbial abundance and composition. Domene et al., 2015, reported that with the rise of corn stover biochar rates from 0 to 14%, microbial population augmented by 5–56% [123]. The same group also reported that after 30tha−1 biochar addition, microbial population was found to increase from 366.1 to 730.5 μgCg−1 [124]. The increase in the microbial population may be due to enhanced water retention and aeration [125,126], higher nutrient availability [127], and less competition [128]. Biochar addition to soil was also found to cause changes in microbial community structure and composition. As compared to unmodified soil, biochar-rich terra preta soils showed an increase in bacterial diversity by 25% [129]. Though, in terra preta and a biochar-amended temperate soil, less diversity of archaea was reported as compared to the unmodified soils [130]. Further, the biological activity of soil can be evaluated by several indicators like metabolism rates and different enzymes. It was found that with the application of biochar, soil dehydrogenase activity increased from 2.75 to 8.96 mgTPFkg−124h−1 [131], and phosphomonoesterase activity increased by 70.8% [132]. All these studies prove the beneficial effects of biochar on the soil.

Biochar has been considered as a slow-release carrier and also prevents the fertilizers leaching in the soil. In the context of slow-release carrier, recently co-composting was performed with biochar at a rate of 2% (w/w) load [133]. The biochar was found to interact with phosphate and nitrate during composting and lead to their controlled release. This mechanism of controlled nutrient release was found to increase the Chenopodium quinoa biomass production by ∼300%. Whereas, 60% decrease in biomass took place with the application of biochar without co-composting. Similarly, the soil amended with co-composted biochar and pure compost were tested for the controlled release of nitrate and ammonium fertilizers [134]. The extraction of nitrate from the biochar-amended soil was found to be 30% completed after 167 h, whereas for pure compost, extraction of nitrate was completed after 1 h only. In the context of leaching, the biochar was directly applied to the soil in a different ratio and nitrate leaching behaviour was followed in the soil at a depth of 15 cm [135]. In total, the biochar application zone reduced the nitrate leaching as compared to the soil without the biochar application. The mechanism behind this anti-leaching property involves the high adsorption ability of biochar.

As fertilizer plays an important role in crop production, pesticide plays an important role in crop protection. Coming to the biochar based carbon platform in the pesticide delivery, initially, the biochar and biosilica (BCS) of 50–200 nm size range and 1–200 nm pores size was obtained from the ash of waste straw and used for CPF application [136]. Here, an emulsion was prepared with ∼200 mg CPF in 950 μL ether, 50 μL Tween, and 40 mL water at 80–400 mg BCS; showed 0.5 times binding on the leaf surface. Compared to the conventional formulation, the addition of BCS proportionally improved the adhesion against washing, leaching, and volatilization. At the rate of highest BCS addition, ∼2–2.5 times improvement in adhesion was observed, which was attributed to the ability of the BCS to retain in the porous leaf surface and binding potential with CPF.

With more advancement, biochar was also employed as a photothermal agent for the light-triggered release of glyphosate herbicide at the field level [137]. In this study, a composite of porous biochar packed with high surface attapulgite, glyphosate, and ammonium carbonate was prepared using Φ360 pelletizer fed. To enhance the loading efficiency, the composite was coated with cellulose and silicon oil. The biochar was found to transduce the heat locally under NIR laser irradiation, which leads to the release of ammonia and CO2 gas from the ammonium carbonate. The gas release in turn found to create the pores on the surface and thus cause the release of herbicide. The efficiency of weed control was checked with tifdwarf bermudagrass, which presented laser-triggered weed control. In addition to high adhesion and triggered release, biochar was also found to reduce the herbicide's half-life. In this context, Jing et al. studied the fate of fenoxaprop-ethyl herbicide and its derivative fenoxaprop without and with the addition of 5% rice husk biochar in soils [138]. Biochar was able to reduce the herbicide half-lives by less than 2 days. Further, in the biochar-amended soil, fenoxaprop was reduced over 85% on the 35th day but large amounts of fenoxaprop found remained in the control soil.

We prepared novel biochar using a fungus that was challenged with the gold ionic solution, through incineration under nitrogen flow [4] (Fig. 14 ). Thus obtained biochar was decorated with gold nanoparticles with high stability. This gained interest as generally in the biosynthesis if the nanoparticles were generated intracellularly it is considered inferior as it is not readily available for the application. Here, this composite was found stable for the harsh physical and chemical treatment as the particles are majorly buried in the carbon-graphite matrix. Hence the composite was able to accommodate different lengths of DNA plasmid and tailor it into the host genome for the execution of transgenic plants varying from soft to hard tissue efficiently than conventional biotechniques.

Biochar obtained from agriwaste in the nanocomposite form has enhanced the removal of contaminants from waste water, by the enhanced surface area, functional groups and thermal/mechanical stability in its composite [139].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contribution of other CNMs in sustainable agriculture
ce:section.ce:para: The unique properties of CNMs discussed in above sections allowed successful use in different agriculture activities including production, protection and preservation. This reduces the threat to the environment, since CNMs aided in the preparation of next generation fertilizers, pesticides, herbicides and preservative designing. Thus, composites played an important role in sustainable agriculture, by assuring yield improvement without non-targeted accumulation.
Table 1 summarizes the properties of CNMs that lead to various agricultural applications and Table 2 outlines the broad application of various carbon based nanomaterials in agriculture.

With continuous development in nanoscience and nanotechnology, CNMs have attracted significant attention in agriculture. Unlike metal and polymer NPs, CNMs are cost-effective, and less toxic to plants. The unique features of CNMs discussed in this review have made them futuristic sustainable agent in agricultural field as they would be helpful in maintaining the equilibrium in the environment by targeted and sustained release of fertilizers, pesticides and preservatives. Agro-application demands for such design as wide application often leads to toxicity. In this context, here the carbon with different morphology and bond hybridization used for the agro-application and found efficient than the conventional formulation in the crop production, protection, and preservation application. The articles discussed are with special emphasis on the targeted, controlled, and inherent property-assisted farming practice improvement. Similarly in other carbon materials section with sp2 allotropes also showed targeted application, which contributed for sustainable future.

However, it is important to investigate the amount of CNMs released to the environments and how it affects the living organisms. Another important direction is to understand the interaction between CNMs and plants at molecular level. To achieve this, efficient analytical (Raman spectroscopy) and imaging techniques (SEM, AFM) can be employed for in situ monitoring and imaging. For instance, Yang et al. proposed a surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) method for in Situ monitoring of thiobendazole (pesticide) penetration in spinach leaves [141]. Since agriculture is a very broad area with a huge number of crops (grouped as monocot/dicot; grass family/non-grass family; temperate/tropical; annual/biannual/perennial; irrigate/rainfed, etc) and chemicals (systemic/non-systemic; mobile/fixed; renewable/non-renewable; organic/inorganic, etc.), there is a lot more research needed to reach the stage of recommendation. Especially, the present literature knowledge available is limited to academic research, which needs modification/adoption to the industrial needs for which industry interaction is needed, to have market focus. Like for example, knowledge sharing about the industry's gold standard (specific crop and location) to check the efficiency of the academic products. Apart from these, researchers working on agro-chemicals may also focus on the international standards for the controlled release. For instance, the European standard specifies controlled release as 15% release in 2 days and 75% in the next 28 days, which can be fixed as the research goal [142]. Therefore, more data and detailed study is still required to make CNMs as commercial agent at large scale for sustainable development of agriculture. The Department of Biotechnology, Government of India has given the draft to follow for the agro-chemicals [143].
